info.name: bot_flow
info.description:
Multi-tenant RAG chatbot SaaS API.

Set the collection variables first:
- baseUrl: API base (prod domain or http://localhost:3000)
- adminKey: the ADMIN_API_KEY (guards /admin/*)
- apiKey: a SECRET key (sk_...) for ingest/search/documents/ask
- publicKey: a PUBLISHABLE key (pk_...) for browser/chat (needs an allowed Origin)
- sessionToken: a login session (sess_...) for the /auth/* routes; set automatically by Register / Login
- keyHash: set automatically by List Keys; the target of Revoke Key
- tenantId: a tenant slug (e.g. acme); set automatically by Register / Create Tenant
- uploadUrl: set automatically by Create Upload URL; the presigned PUT target on MinIO :9000
- documentId: a document_id returned by Create Upload URL
- conversationId: optional; paste the conversation_id a prior /ask returned to continue that conversation

Two ways to onboard a tenant: Admin > Create Tenant (needs adminKey), or Auth > Register (self-serve, no admin key). Both return the first sk_ once.

Key rules: /admin/* needs adminKey. /auth/register + /auth/login are public (rate-limited); the other /auth/* routes need a sessionToken. /ingest, /search, /documents (GET+POST) require a SECRET key. /ask + /ask/stream accept secret OR publishable (publishable also requires an allowed Origin header). /health is open.

request.description: Liveness + dependency check (Postgres, Qdrant). No auth.

Create a tenant and mint its first SECRET key (returned once). Requires adminKey. Its test script captures api_key into {{apiKey}} and tenant_id into {{tenantId}} — the key is shown only here, so if you skip this you cannot get it back.

exec: // The secret key is returned exactly once. Capture it now or mint a new one later.

request.description: Mint a key for a tenant. kind = "secret" or "publishable". For publishable, set allowed_origins. Raw key returned once. Requires adminKey.

Self-serve signup: creates a tenant + owner account + session in one transaction, and returns the first SECRET key ONCE. No admin key. Rate-limited. 'slug' is optional (derived from tenant_name when omitted). The test script captures session_token, tenant_id and the one-time api_key.

exec: // session_token and the sk_ are shown once here. Capture both now.
console.log('session + apiKey captured for tenant', d.tenant_id);

request.description: Verify email + password, get a fresh session. Never re-reveals an API key. A wrong email and a wrong password return the SAME 401 (no account-existence oracle). Rate-limited per email. The test script captures session_token.
The account (email) + tenant (id, name) behind the current session. Used by the web app to hydrate the logged-in user.

This tenant's API keys as METADATA only (kind, label, allowed_origins, created_at, key_hash) — never the raw key. The test script captures the newest key's key_hash into {{keyHash}} so Revoke Key can target it.
Mint a key for your own tenant with a session instead of the admin key — the dashboard equivalent of Admin > Mint API Key. kind = 'secret' or 'publishable'. Raw key returned once.

Change a key's allowed_origins. Its `kind` and its hash are immutable (invariant 26). Run List Keys first to populate {{keyHash}}.

Adding a domain must not mean minting a new key: a pk_ is public and expected to be stolen, so rotating it to add 'www.' buys nothing, while forcing a site-wide <script> edit for a one-word change is how tenants end up asking for a wildcard — which would delete the containment entirely. `kind` stays immutable because flipping it would silently turn a published key secret, or a secret key public, under an unchanged snippet.

Behaviour worth knowing (each verified against a live API):
- allowed_origins is REQUIRED. Omitting it is a 422, not a no-op — there is no serde default.
- Origins are canonicalised, not stored as typed: ["HTTPS://Acme.com:443/", "https://acme.com"] both become "https://acme.com", and the duplicate collapses. They are matched against the browser's Origin header by STRING EQUALITY, so a value in a non-canonical shape is not lax — it is dead, and the key 403s forever with nothing in any log to say why.
- A publishable key with an empty list is refused (422). That is not a permissive key, it is one that can answer from nowhere.
- 'null' is never allow-listable — it is what every file:// page and sandboxed iframe sends.
- A hash that is not yours 404s exactly like one that never existed, so this is not an oracle for which keys exist (same rule as invariants 8 and 18).

Returns {key_hash, kind, allowed_origins} — the list echoed back is the CANONICALISED one, which is what GET /auth/keys will show. (Note POST /auth/keys echoes the raw request value instead, so the two responses can disagree for the same input.)

Revoke one of this tenant's keys by its key_hash (run List Keys first to populate {{keyHash}}). Scoped to your tenant, so you can only revoke your own keys. 204 on success, 404 if the hash isn't yours.
Ends the current session (deletes just this token, not all of the account's sessions). 204.

Mint a presigned PUT. The API never sees the file bytes. Returns 201 {document_id, upload_url, method, expires_at}. Then PUT the file straight at upload_url (no auth header on that request — the signature IS the authorisation). Only .pdf, .txt and .md are accepted. Requires a SECRET key. Its test script stores upload_url and document_id as collection variables, so 'Upload File to MinIO' can run straight after it.

exec: // Stash the presigned URL and id so 'Upload File to MinIO' and 'Refresh Upload URL' just work.

request.description:
STEP 2 of the upload. Equivalent to:
    curl -X PUT --upload-file cv-ridwan.pdf "<upload_url>"

This request goes to MinIO on :9000, NOT to the API on :3000. The API never sees the bytes.

Before sending: open the Body tab (it is set to 'binary') and choose your file. Postman does not save the file path in the collection, so you must pick it once per machine.

{{uploadUrl}} is set automatically by the 'Create Upload URL' request's test script — run that first and this URL fills itself in.

Three things that will break it:
  * Body must be 'binary', NOT 'form-data'. form-data wraps your file in a MIME envelope and MinIO stores the envelope, so the worker cannot parse it.
  * Auth must stay 'No Auth'. The presigned signature lives in the query string; an Authorization header on top of it is rejected.
  * The URL expires (PRESIGN_TTL_SECS, default 900s). If you get 403 AccessDenied, run 'Refresh Upload URL' rather than minting a new session.

Expect 200 + an ETag. Then poll 'List Documents' until status goes uploading -> processing -> ready.

Re-mint a presigned URL for a document still 'uploading' or 'expired'. 404 if it already uploaded, or belongs to another tenant — the same response either way, so it cannot be used to probe for existence. Requires a SECRET key.

formdata.description: PDF, TXT, or MD file to ingest

request.description: DEPRECATED: proxies the whole file through the API, buffered in memory. Use 'Create Upload URL' instead. Kept only until clients migrate.
List this tenant's documents with status (RLS-scoped). Requires a SECRET key.

item.name: Chat

request.description:
Raw vector search (debug): returns hits with score/text/document_id. No LLM, and no relevance floor — unlike /ask, it does not refuse when nothing scores well.

AUTH: a secret key OR a session. NOT a publishable key — this route takes `Actor` and calls require_management(), so a pk_ gets 403 (phase 6). Raw retrieval is not "asking a question", and invariant 15 says a pk_ may only ask questions: the gate is what makes that sentence true, rather than a claim the code quietly contradicted.

Note what the gate does NOT buy: /ask already hands sources[].text to a pk_, so this was never a confidentiality boundary. It bounds SPEND — every query is a billed /embeddings call against EMBEDDING_API_KEY — and the route is now rate limited on the tenant's bucket, the same one /ask draws from.

Turn 1 of a conversation. Send NO conversation_id and the server mints one, returning it as `conversation_id`. The test script captures it into {{conversationId}}.

An empty string or null is treated the same as omitting the field.

Grounded RAG answer as a single JSON blob: `answer` is plain prose and carries NO [n] markers — the model is forbidden from writing them — and `sources[]` is the structured citation list. `sources[].index` is 1-based and is the only way to map the answer back to a passage, so never renumber it (invariant 5).

AUTH: a secret key, a publishable key, OR a session (invariant 27) — no gate. With a publishable key, also send an Origin header matching the key's allowed_origins. Rate limited per tenant, not per credential, so a dashboard question and a widget question share one bucket.

exec: // Stash the conversation so 'Ask (continue conversation)' resolves pronouns against it.

request.description:
Turn 2+. Passes {{conversationId}}, captured by the 'Ask' request above, so the server rewrites a context-dependent question into a standalone one before retrieval.

Run 'Ask' first (e.g. "who is ridwan?"), then this. Without the conversation_id the pronoun "his" retrieves nothing and you get 'Sorry, I couldn't find any relevant information.'

AUTH: a secret key, a publishable key, OR a session (invariant 27) — no gate. With a publishable key, also send an Origin header matching the key's allowed_origins. Rate limited per tenant, not per credential, so a dashboard question and a widget question share one bucket.

Server-Sent Events: a 'conversation' event first (the conversation_id), then 'sources', then incremental 'token' events, then 'done'.

Send no conversation_id to start a new conversation. Postman cannot run a test script against a stream, so copy the id from the first `conversation` event and paste it into the {{conversationId}} collection variable to continue.

AUTH: a secret key, a publishable key, OR a session (invariant 27) — no gate. With a publishable key, also send an Origin header matching the key's allowed_origins. Rate limited per tenant, not per credential, so a dashboard question and a widget question share one bucket.

Two things about the frames that are easy to get wrong. An LLM failure yields a single 'error' event carrying a FIXED string — the gateway's own error body goes to the API log and never to a client (invariant 16), because for a pk_ widget this frame reaches a stranger's browser. And 'done' carries no `data:` field at all, so a browser EventSource — which drops any event with an empty data buffer, per the WHATWG spec — would never fire it. Read the stream with fetch, not EventSource.

item.description:
Who may call these, and why it differs per route.

/ask and /ask/stream take the `Actor` extractor and gate NOTHING: a secret key, a publishable key, or a login session all work (invariant 27). That is not a widening. A pk_ is printed in public page source and is expected to be stolen — 'it can only ask questions' is precisely the containment that makes it safe to print — so these are, by design, the routes the weakest credential in the system may reach. Admitting the stronger ones adds no exposure.

/search is the exception, and in the opposite direction to the one you might guess: it also takes `Actor`, but it CALLS require_management(), so a secret key or a session pass and a publishable key gets 403. Raw retrieval is not asking a question. So the three chat routes do not share an auth model — /ask admits everything, /search refuses the weakest.

For any publishable key, send an Origin header that exactly matches one of the key's allowed_origins, or you get a 403. Rate limiting is keyed on the tenant, not on the credential, so the dashboard, the widget and /search all draw on one bucket.

request.description:
Dev shortcut: synchronously embed + index short raw texts (no file/parse/chunk). Requires a SECRET key, and rate limited on the tenant's bucket (phase 6) — it is a billed /embeddings call like any other.

Not a supported path: it writes vectors with random ids and no document_id, so re-ingesting the same text duplicates them and they can never be listed, re-indexed or removed. See 'Known state & debt' in CLAUDE.md.
